Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Production Of Medical Records In Compliance With Subpoena
Off Calendar Dec-03-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTs MARC LEVINSON, AARON LEVINSON, AMY SOSNICK, VIKING SERVICES CORPORATION, JULIE JEREMY's Motion To Compel Production Of Medical Records In Compliance With Subpoena is DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE. The motion cites CCP 1987.1 and 1987.2, which relate to compliance with subpoenas. CCP 2025.480(c) requires notice of a motion to compel records at a deposition, including by subpoena, be given to "all parties and to the deponent." Deponent One Medical was not served (reply at 6). Thus, the motion is defective. Defendants' argument that the motion seeks to compel Plaintiff to authorize One Medical to produce records-rather than to compel One Medical to comply with the subpoena-is not well taken. The motion does not cite, and the Court is unaware of, authority that would permit the Court to order/enjoin Plaintiff to execute such a release. To issue an injunction, remedies at law must be insufficient. C.f. Dept. of Fish & Game v. Anderson-Cottonwood Irrigation Dist. (1992) 8 Cal.App.4th 1554, 1564-65; Code of Civ. Pro. sec. 526(a). Because CCP 1985.3 and 1987.1 permit a motion to compel, Defendants have sufficient remedies at law. Additionally, the subpoena is overbroad. The request for "all" medical records is improper, especially considering the apparent willingness to withdraw some claims. CCP 1987.1(a) (court may protect against unreasonable violations of privacy). In Davis v. Superior Court (1992) 7 Cal.App.4th 1008, 1015, the court set out a framework for analyzing requests for medical records where there is a question about whether conditions are at issue. Where "directly relevant to the issues of pain and suffering associated with the physical injuries [plaintiff] sustained," records related to mental/emotional health may be discoverable. Id. at 1017. A plaintiff may avoid discovery of such records by introducing evidence that "establishe[s] that the records do not concern treatment for the injuries for which she claims damages." Ibid. Here, the parties should meet and confer to narrow the scope of the subpoena, and if they do not agree, Plaintiff should execute an appropriate stipulation or declarations limiting what she is seeking.

JPT to review prevailing party's proposed order.

Judge Pro Tem: Scott Borrowman, Clerk: S. Le, Not reported. =(302/JPT)
